edges.description: PRPS1 loss-of-function variants reduce phosphoribosylpyrophosphate synthetase 1 enzymatic activity.
Reduced PRS-I activity impairs purine biosynthesis and lowers downstream purine metabolites.
PRPS1-related nucleotide depletion is associated with developmental impairment in affected males.
The PRPS1 deficiency phenotype includes ataxia, consistent with cerebellar-system vulnerability downstream of nucleotide depletion.
PRPS1 deficiency is associated with sensorineural hearing loss, modeled here as auditory hair-cell vulnerability.
PRPS1 deficiency is associated with optic atrophy, modeled here as optic pathway vulnerability.
PRPS1 deficiency affects immune-cell function and is associated with recurrent severe respiratory infections.
Neurodevelopmental impairment manifests clinically as global developmental delay.
Severe central nervous system involvement includes early hypotonia.
Cerebellar dysfunction manifests as progressive ataxia in Arts syndrome.
Auditory hair-cell dysfunction produces early sensorineural hearing impairment.
Optic pathway dysfunction manifests clinically as optic atrophy.
Optic pathway dysfunction in the PRS deficiency spectrum can include retinal dystrophy.
Immune-cell dysfunction contributes to recurrent infections in severe PRPS1 deficiency.
Low PRS-I activity directly reports the enzyme-deficiency node in the Arts syndrome pathograph.
Low urinary hypoxanthine reports the impaired purine-biosynthesis branch downstream of PRS-I deficiency.
Low serum uric acid reports impaired purine biosynthesis downstream of PRS-I deficiency.
Replenishment of erythrocyte adenosine and guanosine nucleotides reports bypass of the purine nucleotide depletion branch.

nodes.description: Progressive cerebellar ataxia is a hallmark neurologic manifestation of Arts syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X‐linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline, often resulting in death before 5 years of age.

nodes.description: Auditory hair cells have high metabolic demands and are vulnerable to PRS-I deficiency, explaining the severe sensorineural hearing impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Association of PRPS1 Mutations with Disease Phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: severe PRS-I deficiency (Arts syndrome) present with peripheral or optic neuropathy, prelingual progressive sensorineural hearing loss, and central nervous system impairment

nodes.description: Cerebellar neurons are vulnerable to reduced nucleotide supply, providing a mechanistic basis for the progressive ataxia in Arts syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X‐linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline, often resulting in death before 5 years of age.

evidence.reference_title: Co-therapy with S-adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside improves t-cell survival and function in Arts Syndrome (PRPS1 deficiency).
evidence.snippet: Treatment of a 3-year old boy with S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) replenished erythrocyte purine nucleotides of adenosine and guanosine, while SAM and nicotinamide riboside co-therapy further improved his clinical phenotype as well as T-cell survival and function.

readouts.interpretation: Replenishment of erythrocyte adenosine and guanosine nucleotides reports bypass of the purine nucleotide depletion branch.

nodes.description: Global developmental delay is part of the severe childhood neurodevelopmental presentation.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X‐linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline, often resulting in death before 5 years of age.

nodes.description: Early hypotonia reflects severe central nervous system involvement in Arts syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X‐linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline, often resulting in death before 5 years of age.

nodes.description: Purine depletion can impair immune-cell function and contributes to the recurrent infection phenotype observed in severe PRPS1 deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X‐linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline, often resulting in death before 5 years of age.

nodes.description: Purine shortage disrupts neuronal development and function, contributing to the severe developmental phenotype of Arts syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X‐linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline, often resulting in death before 5 years of age.

nodes.description: Nicotinamide riboside has been reported in combination with SAMe in Arts syndrome and is mechanistically relevant because PRPS1 deficiency affects NAD-related metabolism.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Supplementation of the purine and NAD pathways outside of PRPP‐dependent reactions is a logical approach and has been reported in a handful of patients, two with S‐adenosylmethionine (SAMe) and one with SAMe and nicotinamide riboside (NR).

nodes.description: Optic atrophy contributes to the visual impairment observed in Arts syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X‐linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline, often resulting in death before 5 years of age.

nodes.description: The optic pathway is affected by reduced nucleotide synthesis, leading to optic atrophy and visual impairment.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X‐linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline, often resulting in death before 5 years of age.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Phospho‐ribosyl‐pyrophosphate synthetase 1 (PRPS1) deficiency is secondary to loss of function variants in PRPS1.

nodes.description: Pathogenic PRPS1 variants reduce phosphoribosylpyrophosphate synthetase 1 function and initiate the PRPS1 deficiency cascade.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Phospho‐ribosyl‐pyrophosphate synthetase 1 (PRPS1) deficiency is secondary to loss of function variants in PRPS1.

nodes.description: Loss of PRPS1 function lowers PRS-I enzyme activity and limits nucleotide precursor availability in high-demand tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Hearing loss and PRPS1 mutations: Wide spectrum of phenotypes and potential therapy.
evidence.snippet: Lower residual activity in PRS-I leads to a more severe clinical manifestation.

evidence.reference_title: Arts syndrome is caused by loss-of-function mutations in PRPS1.
evidence.snippet: Both mutations result in a loss of phosphoribosyl pyrophosphate synthetase 1 activity, as was shown in silico by molecular modeling and was shown in vitro by phosphoribosyl pyrophosphate synthetase activity assays in erythrocytes and fibroblasts from patients.

evidence.reference_title: X-linked Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, Arts syndrome, and prelingual non-syndromic deafness form a disease continuum: evidence from a family with a novel PRPS1 mutation.
evidence.snippet: Enzymatically, PRS-I activity was undetectable in the index subject, reduced in his less affected sister, and normal in his unaffected mother.

readouts.interpretation: Low PRS-I activity directly reports the enzyme-deficiency node in the Arts syndrome pathograph.

nodes.description: Reduced PRS-I activity constrains PRPP-dependent purine, pyrimidine, and NAD-pathway nucleotide synthesis, creating downstream metabolic stress in neurons, cochlear hair cells, optic pathway tissue, and immune cells.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: This enzyme generates phospho‐ribosyl‐pyrophosphate (PRPP), which is utilized in the synthesis of purines, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD), and NAD phosphate (NADP), among other metabolic pathways.

evidence.reference_title: Co-therapy with S-adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside improves t-cell survival and function in Arts Syndrome (PRPS1 deficiency).
evidence.snippet: PRPS1 is an initial and essential step for the synthesis of the nucleotides of purines, pyrimidines, and nicotinamide.

nodes.description: Recurrent infections are common in severe PRPS1 deficiency and contribute to morbidity.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X‐linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline, often resulting in death before 5 years of age.

nodes.description: Retinal dystrophy has been described within the PRPS1 deficiency spectrum together with optic atrophy.

evidence.reference_title: Phosphoribosylpyrophosphate Synthetase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: progressive ophthalmologic involvement (retinal dystrophy and optic atrophy)

nodes.description: SAM supplementation is the best-described reported intervention for Arts syndrome and has been associated with stabilization or improvement.

evidence.reference_title: Association of PRPS1 Mutations with Disease Phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: Currently, purine replacement via S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) supplementation in patients with Arts syndrome appears to improve their condition.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: All patients had stability or improvement of symptoms, suggesting that SAMe and NR can be a treatment option in Arts syndrome, though further studies are warranted.

nodes.description: Severe, early-onset sensorineural hearing impairment is a core feature of Arts syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: S‐adenosylmethionine and nicotinamide riboside therapy in Arts syndrome: A case report and literature review
evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X‐linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline, often resulting in death before 5 years of age.

evidence.reference_title: Arts syndrome is caused by loss-of-function mutations in PRPS1.
evidence.snippet: impaired purine biosynthesis, which is supported by the undetectable hypoxanthine in urine and the reduced uric acid levels in serum from patients.

readouts.interpretation: Low serum uric acid reports impaired purine biosynthesis downstream of PRS-I deficiency.

evidence.snippet: impaired purine biosynthesis, which is supported by the undetectable hypoxanthine in urine and the reduced uric acid levels in serum from patients.
